name: Japan
tactical_document:
# Japan — Tactical Profile

## Identity & Philosophy
Hajime Moriyasu's Japan is Asia's gold standard: a positional, technical, intensely-pressing side that has dismantled Germany and Spain in recent World Cup memory and dominated AFC qualifying with the goal difference of a European top side. The identity is "European football played at Japanese tempo" — every pass has a purpose, every press is choreographed, and every transition is a five-pass move rather than a hopeful long ball. Moriyasu's group has matured into a confident outfit that no longer parks the bus against elite opposition; they go toe-to-toe. The squad has absorbed real blows — captain and midfield destroyer Wataru Endō retired from international duty (Ko Itakura inherits the armband), winger Kaoru Mitoma is out with a hamstring, and now talisman Takefusa Kubo is sidelined by a knee injury that rules him out of the Round of 32. Japan finished Group F as runners-up: they twice came from behind to draw the Netherlands 2-2, dismantled Tunisia 4-0 behind a brace from Ayase Ueda, then drew Sweden 1-1 to secure second on goal difference behind the Dutch. Now they face Brazil in the Round of 32 in Houston. The mentality is unbroken: dominate the ball, run opponents into the ground, and bury chances early and late.

## Formation
- Shape: **3-4-2-1** — Moriyasu's signature back-three system. Three centre-backs, two flying wingbacks, a double pivot, two free "shadow strikers" behind a lone centre-forward. In settled possession it morphs into a **3-2-5** (the wingbacks push high, the pivot splits); out of possession it drops into a compact **5-4-1** mid-block. Against the Netherlands Moriyasu flexed this into a back-four block at moments, but the base identity remains the back three.
- Role mapping (roster order in `japan.yaml`):
  - index 0: GK — **Zion Suzuki** — modern sweeper-keeper, comfortable starting the build-up with feet, sprints out to clear long balls behind the high line.
  - index 1: LCB — **Hiroki Itō** — left-footed ball-playing centre-back on the left of the back three, steps into midfield to break lines and covers behind the left wingback.
  - index 2: CCB — **Ko Itakura** — the new captain and central anchor of the back three, the calmest passer on the team, organises the line and steps up to break lines with vertical passes.
  - index 3: RCB — **Takehiro Tomiyasu** — Japan's most complete defender, right of the back three; aggressive stepper out of possession, can play every position across the back.
  - index 4: LWB — **Keito Nakamura** — left wingback, the engine on the flank: gives width going forward, whips in early crosses, sprints back to make a back five out of possession. Scored Japan's first equaliser against the Netherlands.
  - index 5: DM — **Kaishu Sano** — the ball-winning screen in front of the back three, the role vacated by Endō. High-energy destroyer with elite stamina (17); tackles, recovers, recycles, and almost never crosses the halfway line.
  - index 6: CM — **Daichi Kamada** — the progressive, line-breaking half of the pivot and Japan's chief central creator. Takes the ball off the back three, turns into space, threads through-balls, and arrives late in the box — his deflected header completed the comeback against the Netherlands.
  - index 7: RWB — **Yukinari Sugawara** — right wingback, an athletic full-back by trade with elite stamina (17); overlaps relentlessly, hugs the touchline, delivers early low crosses, and sprints back to make a back five out of possession.
  - index 8: LAM — **Ao Tanaka** — left-sided shadow striker, deputising in the free role with Kubo out. A press-resistant box-to-box midfielder who carries through the lines, links play, and arrives late into the box. Less of a touchline dribbler than Kubo, more of a central combiner.
  - index 9: CF — **Ayase Ueda** — the lone 9, presses from the front, holds the ball up, finishes inside the six-yard box. A classic centre-forward and Eredivisie top scorer, not a false 9.
  - index 10: RAM — **Ritsu Doan** — right-sided shadow striker with a winger's instincts; drifts between the lines, tucks inside onto his left foot to shoot, the team's leading goalscorer in qualifying. Late runs into the box.

## Style of Play

### Build-up
Patient short build-up out of a **back three**: Itakura sits central while Hiroki Itō and Tomiyasu split wide, and Sano-Kamada form the double pivot in front, giving a **3-2** base. The wingbacks (Nakamura left, Sugawara right) push high to pin the opposition fullbacks, turning the shape into a **3-2-5**. Suzuki is the eleventh outfielder — always offering a back-pass option. Japan is utterly comfortable playing through the first line of pressure: 60-70% possession against most opponents. Sano shields, Kamada is the line-breaker, and the CBs (especially Itakura and Itō) are licensed to carry into midfield. With Kubo absent, Tanaka drops to combine in the left half-space and the first switch of play looks to find him or Doan between the lines.

### Pressing
**Intense, coordinated, high block** — Japan presses from the front whenever the opposition GK touches the ball. Ueda curves his run to lock the GK onto one side; Tanaka and Doan cut the passing lanes to the centre-backs; the wingbacks Nakamura and Sugawara jump the opposition fullbacks; Sano and Kamada step up to win the second ball. The press trigger is a back-pass to the GK or a CB taking a heavy first touch. When the press breaks, Japan does NOT chase — they retreat into a compact **5-4-1** mid-block and start again. Stamina is a national strength — every outfielder is rated 15+ in stamina.

### Defensive shape
Compact **5-4-1**: the wingbacks Nakamura and Sugawara drop to form a back five, Tanaka and Doan tuck into a midfield four alongside Sano/Kamada, Ueda leads the line alone. The back three holds a medium-high line — Itakura and Tomiyasu step up aggressively to compress space while Itō covers. Tomiyasu rarely gets dribbled past 1v1. Japan concedes few clear chances because the block is narrow (15 units between the back line and the midfield four) and the second balls are won by Sano.

### Wide play
Asymmetric. **Left side:** Tanaka drifts inside off the half-space to combine centrally, leaving Nakamura to provide the width and the overlap as the natural touchline runner. **Right side:** Sugawara overlaps wide and attacks the outside, while Doan tucks inside off him onto his left foot to shoot; the right of the back three (Tomiyasu) covers the channel behind. The chief creative axis is Kamada–Tanaka–Doan; final-ball patterns flow through the half-spaces with one-touch combinations.

### Final third
Combination football inside the box: cut-backs to Doan arriving late, Doan's left-foot curler from the right, Ueda's near-post header from a Nakamura or Sugawara cross, Kamada arriving late from deep. Japan does NOT shoot from distance; everything is engineered for a clean angle inside 18 yards. When transitions arrive, Doan and Tanaka sprint diagonally toward the opposite post; Sugawara provides the outlet run down the right.

## Set Pieces
- Attacking corners: **Doan** in-swingers from the right, **Kamada** out-swingers from the left. Primary targets: Tomiyasu (near post), Itakura (penalty spot), Ueda (back post).
- Defending corners: hybrid — four zonal markers across the six-yard line, three man-markers (Sano tracks the most dangerous runner), two short-corner blockers.
- Free kicks: Kamada direct from central range, Doan from the right half-space (left foot), Nakamura from the left.
- Penalties: **Ueda** primary, **Doan** secondary, **Sugawara** tertiary.

## decide() Decision Priorities
1. **If my role == "GK" (index 0, Zion Suzuki, #1) and the ball is in the opposition half:** Sweep up to 28 units out of the box. If a long ball is played in behind, sprint to clear.
2. **If my player_id ends with "_5" (DM Sano, #6):** When my team has the ball, NEVER move beyond the halfway line — stay as the deepest midfielder. When the opposition transitions, sprint to tackle the ball-carrier within 6 units.
3. **If my player_id ends with "_8" (LAM Tanaka, #17) and I have the ball in the left half-space:** Carry centrally to combine, then Pass to the index-10 Doan (late runner) or the index-9 Ueda; Shoot if angle < 30° and distance < 23. Arrive late into the box when an attack develops.
4. **If my player_id ends with "_3" (RCB Tomiyasu, #22) and team_phase == "attacking":** Hold the right side of the back three and cover the channel behind the index-7 Sugawara — do NOT push beyond the halfway line. Pure security on the right.
5. **If my player_id ends with "_7" (RWB Sugawara, #19) and I have the ball on the right with no defender within 4 units:** Overlap down the touchline and deliver an early low cross toward the index-9 Ueda (near post) or the index-10 Doan (cut-back).
6. **If my role == "FWD" (index 9, Ayase Ueda, #18) and the ball is at the opposition GK's feet:** Sprint diagonally to press the GK, curving the run to block the pass to the right centre-back.
7. **If team_phase == "transition_defense":** Counter-press within 5 ticks. Anyone within 6 units of the ball-carrier tackles immediately.
8. **If my player_id ends with "_10" (RAM Doan, #10) and the ball is in the opposition half:** Position myself between the lines, 5-10 units behind the index-9 Ueda, in the half-space opposite to the index-8 Tanaka. With the ball and no defender within 4 units, cut onto my left foot and Shoot if distance < 20.
9. **If my player_id ends with "_4" (LWB Nakamura, #13) and the index-8 Tanaka has drifted inside:** Sprint to the touchline as the natural width-giver — overlap automatic when Tanaka tucks in. Out of possession, drop to form a back five.
10. **If my player_id ends with "_6" (CM Kamada, #15) and my team has the ball in midfield:** Receive off the back three or the index-5 Sano, turn forward into space, and play the line-breaking pass to a shadow striker (index 8 Tanaka / index 10 Doan) or carry into the half-space; arrive late into the box when an attack develops down either flank.
11. **If team is leading by 2+ goals and minute > 75:** Drop into a low **5-4-1** — the wingbacks index-4 Nakamura and index-7 Sugawara sit as full-time defenders; recycle possession from the back.
12. **If my role == "DEF" and my player_id ends with "_2" (CCB Itakura, #4, captain) with the ball and no opponent within 8 units:** Carry forward into midfield (Pass only when an opponent steps to me).
13. **Set-pieces in the attacking third within 30 units:** Defer dead-ball to the index-6 Kamada (central) or index-10 Doan (right half-space).

## Key Player Notes
- **Kamada (6):** The creative hub of the midfield. With Endō gone, Kamada's progressive passing and late box arrivals (he scored the 89th-minute equaliser vs the Netherlands) carry Japan's central threat. Pairs with Sano's destruction to form a balanced pivot, and inherits chief set-piece duties with Kubo out.
- **Sano (5):** The screen in front of the back three, inheriting Endō's positional discipline. He does not move past midfield; his ball-winning and stamina (17) are the foundation of Japan's defensive solidity.
- **Itakura (2):** The new captain and the calmest passer in the back three. Organises the line, steps out to break pressure, and is a primary aerial target at attacking set pieces. Came off early vs Sweden but expected to start vs Brazil.
- **Tanaka (8):** Deputises in the free role with Kubo injured. Less a touchline dribbler, more a press-resistant central combiner who carries through the lines and arrives late in the box — Japan loses a little flair but gains midfield control.
- **Tomiyasu (3):** The most tactically intelligent defender. Anchors the right of the back three; can play every position across the back line if needed.
- **Doan (10):** A leading scorer, deployed as the right-sided shadow striker and now Japan's chief attacking talisman with Kubo absent. Cuts inside, shoots early. His finishing (15) is elite for an Asian wide player.
- **Sugawara (7):** The right wingback, an athletic overlapping full-back with elite stamina (17). Stretches the pitch and supplies Ueda with early low crosses.
- **Nakamura (4):** Versatile flank weapon who scored Japan's first goal against the Netherlands; gives the left its width and an early-cross supply.
- **Suzuki (0):** The modern sweeper-keeper. His comfort with the ball at his feet unlocks Japan's back-three build-up.

## Tournament Mindset
Japan arrives at the knockout stage believing they can reach the quarter-finals or further — and the squad has earned the right. The mentality is no longer "park the bus and counter"; it is "out-press the opponent." Having finished Group F as runners-up — a gritty 2-2 comeback against the Dutch, a 4-0 demolition of Tunisia, a hard-fought 1-1 with Sweden — they now meet Brazil in the Round of 32 in Houston. This is the biggest test of the cycle: against the world's most talented attacking transition team, Japan cannot be cavalier. They will still want the ball, but Sano's screen and the back three's discipline must be airtight against Brazilian counters; the wingbacks Nakamura and Sugawara must pick their moments to bomb forward. Even shorn of Endō, Mitoma and now Kubo, Japan's stamina is their hidden superpower — they will run opponents into the 80th minute and find a goal late, as the Netherlands learned. Discipline ratings are uniformly 14+ — they will not get red-carded. Belief is high; the plan is to suffocate Brazil's rhythm, strike on the break or from a set piece, and take the game deep where Japan's fitness becomes decisive.
